description:
You are an integration-platform developer who needs a quick, repeatable way to
    collect “what-shape-is-my-box-in?” information before and after running a set
    API tests.  
    Create a tiny “one-shot” diagnostic kit in your home directory.

    1.  Make a new directory called
            /home/user/system_monitor
        (create the parent directories if they do not already exist).

    2.  Inside that directory create an executable Bash script named
            /home/user/system_monitor/run_diag.sh

        a.  The script must write **all** of its diagnostic output to a file called  
                /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log  
            and overwrite that file on every run (do not append).

        b.  The script must run exactly the following five *system* commands, in
            this exact order:
               1) date
               2) hostname
               3) uptime
               4) df -h
               5) free -m

        c.  For simple machine parsing, surround the output of each command with
            a header line that is composed of three equal signs, one space,
            an all-caps token that names the command, one space, and three equal
            signs, **followed by a newline**.  
            Example header for the date command:
                === DATE ===

        d.  The required on-disk format of latest_report.log is therefore:

            === DATE ===
            <single-line output of the date command>

            === HOSTNAME ===
            <single-line output of the hostname command>

            === UPTIME ===
            <single-line output of the uptime command>

            === DISK_USAGE ===
            <multi-line output of df -h command>

            === MEMORY_USAGE ===
            <multi-line output of free -m command>

            •  There must be a **blank line (newline)** after every block so that
               each header section is separated by an empty line.
            •  Section titles must be exactly as shown above.

    3.  After creating the script, execute it once so that
            /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log
        is populated.

    4.  When you are finished, both of the following files must exist:

        •  /home/user/system_monitor/run_diag.sh   (must be executable)
        •  /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log

    5.  A quick self-check you can do in the terminal before declaring success:

        a.  cat /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log  
            should show the five header blocks in the exact order, each followed
            by at least one line of command output and an empty line.

        b.  wc -l /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log  
            will return a number ≥ 11 (because there are 10 header/blank lines
            plus at least one line of actual data).

    Automated grading will:

        •  Inspect the contents and executable bit of run_diag.sh.  
        •  Execute run_diag.sh to make sure it regenerates latest_report.log
          without error.  
        •  Verify that latest_report.log contains the five header blocks in the
          correct order, that each block is followed by at least one non-blank
          line of output, and that blocks are separated by exactly one blank
          line.

    Do NOT hard-code sample outputs—run the commands live so the file reflects
    the true state of the container at execution time.  The only fixed part of
    the log file is the header/blank-line structure defined above.
truth:
<!--
        The grader will rely on the following expectations.

        1.  Directory /home/user/system_monitor must exist.
        2.  /home/user/system_monitor/run_diag.sh  must:
              * be executable (mode 755 or 700 are both acceptable).
              * begin with a shebang (#!/bin/bash or #!/usr/bin/env bash).
              * redirect all output (both stdout and, optionally, stderr) to
                /home/user/system_monitor/latest_report.log, overwriting it.
              * contain, in order, the commands:
                    date
                    hostname
                    uptime
                    df -h
                    free -m
              * precede each command with:
                    echo "=== <TOKEN> ==="
                where <TOKEN> is DATE, HOSTNAME, UPTIME, DISK_USAGE, MEMORY_USAGE
                respectively.
              * include a single echo "" (blank line) **after** each command’s
                output block (five blank lines total).

        3.  After the candidate runs the script once, the grader will:
              * stat and cat latest_report.log to confirm it was freshly written.
              * Grep for the five headers in the precise order.
              * Check that each header is followed by at least one non-blank line
                before the next header.
              * Confirm exactly one blank line exists between header blocks.

        The actual *contents* of the command outputs are dynamic, so they are not
        hard-coded here; only the structural pattern is enforced.
    -->
name: task_000000_6a6a571e
